findings.title: Backend Dockerfile runs as root
findings.description: The backend Dockerfile has no USER directive, meaning the container runs as root by default. If the container is compromised, an attacker has root access within the container.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker exploits a vulnerability in the FastAPI/Socket.io backend (e.g., via crafted WebSocket messages). 2. The container runs as root, so the attacker has full control inside the container. 3. The backend has read access to Cowrie logs via volume mount, potentially exposing attack data.
findings.impact: Root user in container increases blast radius if container is compromised. Volume mounts (cowrie-logs, geoip) become accessible to attacker.
findings.recommendation: Add `USER nobody` or create a dedicated non-root user in the Dockerfile after installing dependencies. Example: `RUN useradd -m appuser && chown -R appuser /app` then `USER appuser`.
findings.playbook: 1. Update backend/Dockerfile to add a non-root user after dependency installation. 2. Ensure volumes are readable by the app user. 3. Test health check still works. 4. Rebuild and redeploy.

findings.title: No authentication in application layer
findings.description: The FastAPI backend has no authentication mechanism. WebSocket connections are accepted without any authentication. Security relies entirely on nginx HTTP Basic Auth in production, but in development mode (localhost:3000), there's no auth at all.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. In development, any client connecting to ws://localhost:8000 can receive attack data. 2. If nginx auth is misconfigured in production, the WebSocket endpoint is exposed without auth. 3. An unauthenticated attacker could connect to the WebSocket and receive real-time attack data.
findings.impact: Unauthenticated access to attack visualization data. Could expose internal security monitoring to unauthorized users.
findings.recommendation: Add WebSocket authentication. Options: (A) Pass auth token in WebSocket query params and validate on connect, (B) Use Socket.io auth option to pass credentials, (C) Require auth on the /socket.io endpoint at nginx level.
findings.playbook: 1. Implement token-based auth in Socket.io connect handler. 2. Validate token against a configured secret or user store. 3. Reject connections without valid auth. 4. Update nginx config to exempt health endpoint but require auth on WebSocket.

findings.title: No CI/CD workflows configured
findings.description: The project has no CI/CD pipelines. No automated security scanning, dependency updates, or deployment checks. Security issues like vulnerable dependencies or Dockerfile problems must be caught manually.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. A vulnerable dependency is introduced. 2. No automated security scanning runs to catch it. 3. The vulnerable code is deployed to production without review. 4. Attacker exploits the vulnerability.
findings.impact: Security issues can slip through to production. Dependency vulnerabilities go undetected. No automated enforcement of code quality or security standards.
findings.recommendation: Add a GitHub Actions workflow with: (A) npm audit / pip-audit for dependency scanning, (B) CodeQL or Snyk for SAST, (C) Docker build test, (D) Basic unit tests if applicable.
findings.playbook: 1. Create .github/workflows/ci.yml. 2. Add steps for npm audit, pip-audit, and build tests. 3. Configure branch protection to require CI passes. 4. Consider adding Dependabot for automated dependency updates.

findings.title: brace-expansion DoS vulnerability (dev dependency)
findings.description: npm audit reports a moderate severity DoS vulnerability in brace-expansion (CVE-2024-XXXXX). Zero-step sequence causes process hang and memory exhaustion. However, this is a dev dependency (used by ESLint), not production code.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker controls input to a regex pattern using brace-expansion. 2. Crafted input causes catastrophic backtracking. 3. Development process hangs. Note: This affects dev tooling, not the running application.
findings.impact: Development tool DoS. Does not affect production runtime. Low risk since it requires dev-time interaction with malicious input.
findings.recommendation: Run `npm audit fix` in frontend/ directory to update to brace-expansion@1.1.13+ or 5.0.5+.
findings.playbook: 1. Run `cd frontend && npm audit fix`. 2. Verify fix applied. 3. Commit package-lock.json changes.

findings.title: Untrusted data flow from Cowrie logs to WebSocket
findings.description: The backend parses JSON data from Cowrie honeypot logs (untrusted attacker input) and broadcasts it to WebSocket clients. While Pydantic models provide some validation, raw command strings and IP addresses flow through to the frontend without sanitization.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker sends crafted commands to Cowrie honeypot with malicious content. 2. Commands are logged by Cowrie. 3. Backend reads logs and parses to AttackEvent. 4. Data sent via WebSocket to frontend. 5. Frontend displays IP, commands count, etc. Note: React escapes by default, so XSS is unlikely.
findings.impact: Attacker-controlled data (commands, IPs) displayed in UI. React's default escaping mitigates XSS. The data flows through the system but is not executed.
findings.recommendation: The data flow is acceptable for a honeypot visualization tool. Consider: (A) Add max length validation on command strings, (B) Sanitize IP addresses to ensure valid format, (C) Document that attack data is untrusted.
findings.playbook: This is low risk given React's XSS protections. Document the trust boundary. Add input validation if desired for defense-in-depth.
